The research gap is that existing studies on edge resource allocation for the metaverse either lack consideration of human-centric factors affecting Quality of Experience (QoE) or fail to model the dynamic interactions between multiple users in a collaborative environment. Most prior works focus on single-agent or non-cooperative frameworks, ignoring the complex interdependencies among users and the need for adaptive resource allocation in highly dynamic metaverse scenarios. Additionally, there is a shortage of methods that effectively balance QoE optimization with fair resource distribution, especially in environments with varying network conditions and user demands. The integration of graph-based models to capture spatial-temporal relationships between users in metaverse networks has also been underexplored.
Lack of technical frameworks and methodologies for meaningful Metaverse learning, especially in designing intelligent NPCs and addressing computational intensity and training time in RL.
Lack of real-world deployment validation, Limited scalability testing
Existing studies focus on deadline achievement rather than E2E latency fairness. There is a gap in using RL to minimize maximum E2E latency and improve fairness in dynamic network environments.

Most multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) studies are confined to simulation environments, lacking real-world validation.
